title: Observable — Anatomy of a Black Hole
logline: Event horizon, shadow, and accretion disk — what we observe versus what simulations show; EHT and LIGO evidence on file.

brand.title: Observable
brand.subtitle: Upon Tyne Science — evidence before wonder
brand.cta: Follow Observable — one measurement at a time.

science_subject.key_measurement: M87* shadow diameter ~42 μas (EHT 2019); GW150914 chirp mass ~62 M☉
science_subject.visualization_prompt: photorealistic scientific visualization, supermassive black hole with dark central shadow, thin relativistic accretion disk with temperature gradient, Doppler beaming on inclined disk, gravitational lensing of background starfield, photon ring, NOT TO SCALE illustrative geometry, 16:9 astrophysics fidelity

beats.speech_text: What if you crossed a boundary where even light cannot climb back out? That is not science fiction — it is what a black hole does to spacetime.
beats.on_screen: Observable · Anatomy of a Black Hole

beats.speech_text: A black hole is not a hole you fall through. It is mass so compressed that gravity curves space and time into a one-way boundary — the event horizon — with a dark shadow at the center and, often, a blazing accretion disk around it.

In twenty nineteen, the Event Horizon Telescope resolved the shadow of M87's supermassive black hole — forty-two micro-arcseconds wide. LIGO has heard stellar-mass black holes merge, rippling spacetime itself. We observe the shadow and the waves — not the singularity inside.

This visualization shows what general relativity predicts: a dark shadow, a thin relativistic disk with Doppler beaming, and bent starlight around the photon sphere — illustrative geometry, not a photograph.
beats.visualization_prompt: Kerr black hole cross-section with labeled event horizon and photon sphere, inclined accretion disk with temperature gradient and Doppler beaming, lensed background stars, documentary astrophysics fidelity

beats.speech_text: Black holes are cosmic laboratories. They test Einstein in extreme gravity, power quasars across the universe, and remind us that empty-looking sky can hide the most violent objects in nature.

lines: SUBJECT: Anatomy of a Black Hole (BH-ANAT-001)
EVIDENCE: EHT M87* shadow (2019); LIGO GW150914 (2016)
VIZ: Illustrative GR simulation — labeled NOT TO SCALE; not photographic
